lede: Cannes Day 3 crowns Uber Eats' personalisation play as the new Media Grand Prix benchmark, Hermès leads a landmark AI search visibility index, and the industry's first AI Craft Lion forces juries — and luxury CMOs — to prove AI changed the actual outcome.

stories.headline: Cannes Lions Day 3: Uber Eats Wins Media Grand Prix by Collapsing the Funnel — and Carat is Named Media Network of the Year
stories.summary: Uber Eats' 'Build Your Own Super Bowl Commercial' — 1,000+ bespoke AI-assisted pieces of content, $36M in incremental sales, 3.7M new app visits — won the Media Grand Prix on Day 3, June 24. WPP Media's Sindhuja Rai, presiding as jury president, declared the work 'seamlessly united creativity, media, technology, talent, commerce, and personalisation into a single connected experience, effectively collapsing the traditional funnel.' The day delivered 181 total awards across Creative Data, Media, Direct, PR and Social & Creator Lions. Carat was named Media Network of the Year. For luxury media executives, the Uber Eats case is the clearest live proof yet that participation-led media strategy — not reach-led planning — is now the jury benchmark.

stories.headline: Hermès Tops First-Ever AI Search Visibility Index — Over a Third of Luxury Buyers Now Start with AI, Not Google
stories.summary: WWD's editorial coverage (June 22) of the AI Luxury 25 — built by 5W and Haute Living across ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity and Google AI Overviews — reveals Hermès leads at 98.6, with Rolex, Patek Philippe, Cartier and Chanel completing the top tier. Louis Vuitton and Tiffany & Co. land in the top ten. More than a third of luxury buyers now begin product research with AI engines, not Google. Critically, the study finds that AI rewards consistency and archival depth over campaign novelty — brands penalised by frequent creative director changes or sub-brand proliferation. For LVMH's media and data teams, the implication is structural: GEO is no longer optional, and brand-corpus discipline is now a measurable performance lever.

stories.headline: Cannes Lions 2026 Launches AI Craft Subcategory — Juries Now Demand Proof AI Changed the Outcome, Not Just That It Was Used
stories.summary: The debut AI Craft subcategory inside Film Craft Lions at Cannes 2026 marks a structural shift in how the industry evaluates AI-generated creative work. For the first time, entries must demonstrate that AI materially changed the final output — not merely that it was deployed. The festival's awards director Marian Brannelly stated: 'Technology isn't just supporting creativity, it's allowing us to conquer new creative frontiers.' Simultaneously, WPP is exiting its beach presence — a signal that holding groups are redirecting investment away from hospitality toward AI and data product showcases inside the Palais. For luxury creative leaders, the AI Craft bar being set this week will directly inform how agencies brief AI-enabled campaigns for the 2026-27 cycle.

stories.headline: The AI Luxury 25: LVMH's Louis Vuitton and Tiffany & Co. Make the Top Ten — But Kering's Houses Are Absent From the Rankings
stories.summary: The same AI Luxury 25 study (5W/Haute Living, June 9, widely covered this week) delivers a pointed competitive intelligence signal for LVMH's group strategy: Louis Vuitton (tied 6th) and Tiffany & Co. (10th) secured places in the top ten, while no Kering maison appears. The study scores houses on archival depth, citation density, entity clarity, editorial consistency, and retrieval stability across five major AI engines. The finding that AI engines penalise brands with frequent creative director changes — a descriptor that applies to several Kering houses — is directly actionable for LVMH's GEO strategy. The report's central conclusion: luxury is no longer just about the boutique or the campaign; it is about owning the machine's answer to a buyer.

stories.headline: POV Smartglasses Are Going Viral Inside Luxury Boutiques — and Gucci to Patek Philippe Have No Answer
stories.summary: Content creators wearing Meta AI smartglasses are secretly filming point-of-view videos inside luxury boutiques — from Gucci to Patek Philippe — and publishing them to TikTok and Instagram, where they are going viral. Business of Fashion's investigation (May 18) has been gaining renewed traction as Meta launches its own-brand AI glasses this week at Cannes, widening the threat surface. The glasses are nearly indistinguishable from standard eyewear and require no visible recording indicator. Ireland's Data Protection Commission and Italy's privacy regulator have already opened inquiries. For luxury brand and media teams, this is a live reputational and legal risk: the same hardware ecosystem LVMH's retail partners are exploring for client experience is the vector for unauthorised in-store surveillance content at scale.
